title: CAT 2026 — MBA Entrance Exam
meta_description: CAT 2026 — IIM MBA entrance exam with 200+ word details on exam pattern, VARC/DILR/QA sections, cutoff for top IIMs, fees, and preparation strategy.

hero.one_liner: Common Admission Test — the gateway to 20 IIMs and 1,200+ MBA colleges. India's most prestigious management entrance exam.

what_is_it: CAT (Common Admission Test) is conducted annually by one of the 20 IIMs (on rotation basis — IIM Indore conducting CAT 2026) for admission to their MBA/PGDM programs and 1,200+ participating B-schools across India that accept CAT scores. It is the most prestigious management entrance exam in the country, with participation from educational institutions ranging from elite IIMs to strong regional business schools. The exam tests candidates' aptitude in three core areas: Verbal Ability & Reading Comprehension (VARC), Data Interpretation & Logical Reasoning (DILR), and Quantitative Ability (QA). The total exam spans 120 minutes with exactly 40 minutes allocated per section.

CAT percentile (not raw score) determines your ranking among all test takers. For admission to the top 3 IIMs (Ahmedabad, Bangalore, Calcutta), you typically need 99+ percentile. For IIM Lucknow, Indore, Kozhikode: 97-99 percentile. For newer IIMs (Trichy, Ranchi, Raipur, Kashipur, Shillong, Bodh Gaya): 90-97 percentile is sufficient. Many excellent B-schools outside the IIM network offer strong placements with 80+ percentile. The exam uniquely prioritizes aptitude over knowledge — you can prepare effectively in 4-6 months with consistent effort, even alongside full-time work.

What makes CAT distinct among competitive exams is that section-wise time limits are rigid — you cannot switch between sections or reallocate time. This tests not just knowledge but also time management and section strategies. The exam combines Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) and Type-In-The-Answer (TITA) questions, where TITA questions carry no negative marking, making them strategic for risk-averse approaches. Many CAT toppers are working professionals with 2-5 years of experience who prepared 2-3 hours daily. Some notable IIM alumni work for Amazon, Google, Flipkart, and McKinsey — the ROI of CAT to IIM is the highest among Indian educational investments.

The CAT score is valid only for the year of exam — scores cannot be carried forward to next year. However, candidates can attempt CAT multiple times across years. The cutoff for a general category candidate to merely qualify for IIM Ahmedabad interviews is around 99.5 percentile, yet even 95+ percentile candidates get selected at tier-2 IIMs with strong interviews. The selection process post-CAT involves Written Ability Test (WAT) and Personal Interview (PI) where profile matters — work experience, academic background, and leadership qualities are heavily evaluated.

key_details.label: Education Requirement
key_details.value: Bachelor's degree with 50% (45% for SC/ST/PwD). Final year students can apply.

key_details.label: Exam Mode
key_details.value: Computer Based Test at designated exam centers nationwide

key_details.label: Duration
key_details.value: 120 minutes total (40 min per section, cannot switch sections)

key_details.label: Total Questions
key_details.value: 66 questions across 3 sections: VARC (24) + DILR (20) + QA (22)

key_details.label: Marking Scheme
key_details.value: +3 for correct MCQ, -1 for wrong MCQ. TITA questions: +3 for correct, no negative marking

exam_pattern.tier_name: CAT Paper (120 minutes — 3 Sections with Fixed Time)
exam_pattern.description: Each of the 3 sections has a mandatory 40-minute window. You cannot switch between sections or carry over unused time. Section 1: VARC (24 questions), Section 2: DILR (20 questions), Section 3: QA (22 questions). Mix of MCQs and TITA format.

subjects.name: VARC — Verbal Ability & Reading Comprehension (24 Qs: 20 MCQ, 4 TITA)
DILR — Data Interpretation & Logical Reasoning (20 Qs: 16 MCQ, 4 TITA)
QA — Quantitative Ability (22 Qs: 18 MCQ, 4 TITA)

exam_pattern.duration: 120 minutes (40 min Section 1, 40 min Section 2, 40 min Section 3)
exam_pattern.negative_marking: MCQs: -1 for wrong answer. TITA (Type In The Answer): No negative marking. +3 for all correct answers.

extra_sections.heading: VARC Strategy — Reading Comprehension Dominance
content:
Understanding VARC Weightage

Reading Comprehension (RC) accounts for 16-18 out of 24 VARC questions, making it the dominant subsection. Verbal Ability questions (grammar, vocabulary, sentence correction) form only 6-8 questions.
This means your reading speed and comprehension directly determine your VARC score. Many CAT aspirants spend 50% of VARC time on 4-5 RC passages.

RC Practice Method

Read high-quality articles daily from The Economist, Aeon, LiveMint Opinion, Harvard Business Review, or The Guardian. Focus on: (1) Grasping the main argument in 2 sentences, (2) Identifying author's tone (supportive/critical/neutral), (3) Locating specific details without re-reading entire passages, (4) Understanding logical flow between paragraphs.
CAT RC passages average 300-400 words. You have ~7-8 minutes per passage including questions.
Time pressure is deliberate — simulate exam conditions in practice.

extra_sections.heading: DILR Strategy — Diverse Set Types
content:
DILR Unpredictability

DILR is the most unpredictable section — no two exams have identical set types. You might face logic games (seating arrangements, grouping), data tables (charts, graphs requiring calculations), or combination puzzles.
The key is not to over-prepare any single set type but to develop adaptability.

Time Allocation in DILR

With 20 questions in 40 minutes (2 minutes per question), you can attempt only 12-14 questions comfortably. The strategy: (1) Spend 3-4 minutes on a set to determine if it's solvable, (2) If difficult, skip and move, (3) Never spend 8+ minutes on a single set, (4) Solve 3 sets completely (12 questions) at 100% accuracy rather than 5 sets at 60% accuracy.
Your 9-10 percentile gain comes from accurate completion of 3 sets, not from attempt quantity.

extra_sections.heading: Post-CAT Selection Process at IIMs
rows: Your CAT percentile determines whether you get a call for WAT+PI. Typically 80+ percentile = almost guaranteed call, 70-80 = borderline, <70 = rare calls.
30-minute essay or topic-based argument writing. Tests articulation, logical flow, grammar. Score contributes to final merit.
15-20 minute interview. Questions on: why MBA, why IIM, your background, work experience, industry knowledge, current affairs. Behavioral assessment.
Your 10th/12th/graduation marks, work experience years, diversity factors. CAT alone doesn't determine selection.

preparation_tips: Start with a diagnostic mock test to identify your baseline strengths/weaknesses. Most CAT aspirants are naturally strong in 1-2 sections and weak in 1. Allocate preparation time inversely — 40% on your weakest section, 30% on medium, 30% on strongest. This focused approach gains more percentile than balanced preparation.

Reading Comprehension is the efficiency multiplier — 70% of VARC marks from ~3 RC passages. Read 1-2 quality articles daily (The Economist, Aeon) and practice summarizing main arguments in 2 sentences. Develop a 'reading map' while reading — note paragraph themes, author's position, transitions. This prevents re-reading and saves 2-3 minutes per passage.

DILR requires adaptive thinking, not rote learning. Practice diverse set types from LSAT (US law entrance exam) resources, as they offer harder logic puzzles than typical CAT coaching material. The goal: quickly assess if a set is doable in your time window. Solving 3 sets perfectly is worth more than attempting all 5 poorly.

Quantitative Ability: Master 4 core topics (Arithmetic 30%, Algebra 25%, Geometry 20%, Number System 15%) which cover 90% of QA. If geometry is weak, don't despair — you can hit 95+ percentile QA score focusing only on Arithmetic + Algebra. Calculator is not allowed in CAT — mental math and approximation are critical. Practice without calculator for 2 months before exam.

Mock test strategy: Attempt 1 full mock per week from month 3 onwards. Analyze: Why did I get questions wrong? Silly mistakes vs. knowledge gaps? Time management inefficiency? Keep a mistake log by topic. Retake the same 5 mock exams 4 weeks before CAT after 10+ fresh mocks — your score improvement on familiar tests shows your true ceiling.

faqs.question: Can working professionals take CAT while managing a full-time job?
faqs.answer: Absolutely yes. There's no age limit, and many IIM students are working professionals with 2-5 years of experience. In fact, IIMs value work experience — it strengthens your candidacy for shortlisting and interviews. Many CAT 99+ percentile scorers prepared 2-3 hours daily while working. The key: consistent daily effort > sporadic marathon sessions. Early morning (5-7 AM) or post-work (9-11 PM) slots are common among working professionals.

faqs.question: IIM tuition fees are very high. Can I afford it with a loan?
faqs.answer: IIM 2-year MBA fees range from ₹15-28 lakh depending on the IIM. This seems high, but: (1) Education loans cover 100% with no collateral and low 6-7% interest rates, (2) Median placement at top IIMs is ₹25-35 LPA — you repay the loan in 2.5-3 years, (3) ROI calculation: ₹28 lakh invested = ₹35 LPA salary = ₹7 lakh annual return = 25% ROI. This is higher than most investments. Nearly 95% of IIM MBA students take loans.

faqs.question: I scored poorly in CAT attempt 1. Should I retake it next year?
faqs.answer: Yes. No limit on CAT attempts. Most successful candidates take 2-3 attempts. First attempt gives real exam experience and a baseline score. Use this data to calibrate your preparation for the next attempt. Example: If you scored 85 percentile in CAT 1 with weak DILR, spend 3 months focused only on DILR, then attempt CAT 2. Improvement from 85 to 92 percentile is achievable. Don't be discouraged by a single attempt — many 99+ percentile scorers failed in their first attempt.

faqs.question: CAT vs GMAT — which exam for MBA?
faqs.answer: CAT for Indian B-schools (IIMs, ISB, XLRI, FMS, MDI). GMAT for international B-schools (Harvard, Wharton, INSEAD, IIMB's PGPX program). ISB Hyderabad accepts both CAT and GMAT. If your goal is purely Indian MBA, CAT is the proven path. If considering international MBA or global exposure programs, GMAT opens more doors. GMAT is harder conceptually but easier to self-study from home (available year-round). CAT is offered once per year.

faqs.question: Coaching vs Self-Study — which is better for CAT?
faqs.answer: Self-study works if you're disciplined and have strong fundamentals. Coaching helps in: structured curriculum, expert strategy insights for DILR (which is hardest to self-learn), and regular mock tests. Ideal approach: Self-study for 2 months to identify weak areas, then 2-3 months of coaching for those areas, then final 2 months self-study for refinement. Popular CAT coaching: Career Launcher, Byju's, IMS Learning, TIME. Each has strengths. Many toppers use low-cost online subscriptions (₹10-30K) rather than expensive classroom coaching (₹1-3 lakh).

faqs.question: What's the cutoff for admission to the highest-paying MBA job?
faqs.answer: For recruiting companies like McKinsey, Google, Amazon offering ₹25-40 LPA packages: CAT percentile alone doesn't matter. IIM matters. At IIM A/B/C, 85% of students receive offers ≥₹20 LPA. At newer IIMs, 40-50% receive ≥₹15 LPA. However, your major (specialization), interview performance, and internship matter equally. A 90 percentile IIM-A student in a non-core major may earn less than a 95 percentile IIM-L student in IT/Finance. Choose your IIM carefully based on placement data for your desired specialization.

syllabus.subject: Verbal Ability & Reading Comprehension (VARC)
syllabus.topics: Reading comprehension (5 passages, 24 questions), para jumbles, para summary, sentence completion, odd sentence out, critical reasoning

syllabus.subject: Data Interpretation & Logical Reasoning (DILR)
syllabus.topics: Data interpretation (tables, graphs, caselets), logical reasoning (arrangements, games & tournaments, routes & networks, binary logic, constraint-based puzzles)

syllabus.subject: Quantitative Ability (QA)
syllabus.topics: Arithmetic (percentage, profit/loss, SI/CI, time & work, mixtures), algebra, number system, geometry, mensuration, coordinate geometry, trigonometry, probability & combinatorics

study_plan.tasks: CAT preparation needs 8-10 months. Start with arithmetic basics from Arun Sharma. Read 1 editorial daily (The Hindu/Mint) for RC practice. Basic DILR sets from Arun Sharma. Build reading habit — 30 pages/day of any English book.
Advanced quant — geometry, P&C, probability. DILR practice — 2 sets daily (CAT DILR is unpredictable, practice variety). RC speed building — 1 passage in 10 minutes. Start mock tests monthly.
Weekly full mocks (IMS, TIME, CL, Unacademy). Analyze every mock — find time-wasting questions. CAT rewards smart selection — skip hard questions, maximize easy ones. Target: 99%ile needs ~105/198.

Daily mocks in last 30 days. Revise quant formulas. Practice RC under time pressure. DILR — don't spend more than 15 min on any set (move on if stuck). Stay calm — CAT tests temperament more than knowledge.

quiz.question: If the cost price of 20 articles equals selling price of 16 articles, the profit % is:
How many ways can 5 people sit in a row?
